section.title: The Sabbath and the Offering
section.sectionRationale: After the covenant is renewed, construction of the tabernacle finally begins. Moses restates the Sabbath command to the assembled congregation, then calls for a freewill offering, and the people respond with overflowing generosity, presenting Bezalel and Oholiab as the Spirit-gifted craftsmen who will lead the work.
section.thesis: Moses gathers Israel, restates the Sabbath, and calls for a willing offering for the tabernacle, and the people, men and women alike, give so generously and skillfully that the LORD's chosen craftsmen, Bezalel and Oholiab, are ready to begin building.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Sabbath restated to the whole congregation; the call for offerings of materials and skilled labor; the people's overflowing, willing response; and the announcement of Bezalel and Oholiab, filled with the Spirit for the work.

units.label: The Sabbath restated
units.note: a holy day, a Sabbath of complete rest

verses.text: Then Moses assembled the whole congregation of Israel and said to them, “These are the things that the LORD has commanded you to do:
For six days work may be done, but the seventh day shall be your holy day, a Sabbath of complete rest to the LORD. Whoever does any work on that day must be put to death.
Do not light a fire in any of your dwellings on the Sabbath day.”

media.alt: Hands offering gold, yarn, and wood
media.caption: Moses calls for a willing offering to build everything the LORD has commanded.

verses.text: Moses also told the whole congregation of Israel, “This is what the LORD has commanded:
Take from among you an offering to the LORD. Let everyone whose heart is {{willing|willing}} bring an offering to the LORD: gold, silver, and bronze;
blue, purple, and scarlet yarn; fine linen and goat hair;
ram skins dyed red and fine leather; acacia wood;
olive oil for the light; spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense;
and onyx stones and gemstones to be mounted on the ephod and breastpiece.
Let every skilled craftsman among you come and make everything that the LORD has commanded:
the tabernacle with its tent and covering, its clasps and frames, its crossbars, posts, and bases;
the ark with its poles and mercy seat, and the veil to shield it;
the table with its poles, all its utensils, and the Bread of the Presence;
the lampstand for light with its accessories and lamps and oil for the light;
the altar of incense with its poles; the anointing oil and fragrant incense; the curtain for the doorway at the entrance to the tabernacle;
the altar of burnt offering with its bronze grate, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin with its stand;
the curtains of the courtyard with its posts and bases, and the curtain for the gate of the courtyard;
the tent pegs for the tabernacle and for the courtyard, along with their ropes;
and the woven garments for ministering in the holy place, both the holy garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve as priests.”
Then the whole congregation of Israel withdrew from the presence of Moses.
And everyone whose heart stirred him and whose spirit prompted him came and brought an offering to the LORD for the work on the Tent of Meeting, for all its services, and for the holy garments.

So all who had willing hearts, both men and women, came and brought brooches and earrings, rings and necklaces, and all kinds of gold jewelry. And they all presented their gold as a wave offering to the LORD.
Everyone who had blue, purple, or scarlet yarn, or fine linen, goat hair, ram skins dyed red, or articles of fine leather, brought them.
And all who could present an offering of silver or bronze brought it as a contribution to the LORD. Also, everyone who had acacia wood for any part of the service brought it.
Every skilled woman spun with her hands and brought what she had spun: blue, purple, or scarlet yarn, or fine linen.
And all the skilled women whose hearts were stirred spun the goat hair.
The leaders brought onyx stones and gemstones to mount on the ephod and breastpiece,
as well as spices and olive oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense.
So all the men and women of the Israelites whose hearts prompted them brought a freewill offering to the LORD for all the work that the LORD through Moses had commanded them to do.

units.label: Bezalel and Oholiab announced
units.note: filled with the Spirit of God

verses.text: Then Moses said to the Israelites, “See, the LORD has called by name Bezalel son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.
And He has filled him with the Spirit of God, with skill, ability, and knowledge in all kinds of craftsmanship,
to design artistic works in gold, silver, and bronze,
to cut gemstones for settings, and to carve wood, so that he may be a master of every artistic craft.
And the LORD has given both him and Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, the {{ability|ability}} to teach others.

He has filled them with skill to do all kinds of work as engravers, designers, embroiderers in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine linen, and as weavers, as artistic designers of every kind of craft.

willing.gloss: A willing, or generous, heart. This phrase, repeated throughout the chapter, describes the freely stirred motivation behind Israel's giving, offerings given from love and eagerness rather than command or compulsion.

ability.gloss: The ability to teach or instruct. Bezalel and Oholiab were not only gifted craftsmen themselves but given the further gift of passing their skill to others, ensuring the work could be carried out by many hands, not theirs alone.

basic.context: With the covenant renewed, Moses gathers all Israel, restates the Sabbath, and calls for a freewill offering of materials and skilled labor to build the tabernacle. The people respond so generously, men and women alike bringing gold, yarn, wood, and their own skilled handiwork, that Moses can announce Bezalel and Oholiab, filled with God's Spirit, as ready to lead the work.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical account of Israel's actual response to God's call for the tabernacle's construction. Its significance lies not in hidden symbolism but in what it plainly shows: genuine, willing generosity flowing from a people whose hearts had been stirred, contrasted sharply with the golden calf's earlier, misdirected generosity toward an idol.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter repeats nadiv lev, willing or generous heart, again and again, emphasizing that true worship flows from a heart moved, not merely a rule obeyed. Bezalel and Oholiab receive not only skill but horaah, the ability to teach others, so that the Spirit's gifting would multiply through many hands.
basic.typology: The people's overflowing, willing generosity for God's true dwelling place stands in deliberate contrast with their earlier eager giving toward the golden calf, showing that the same generous impulse can be directed rightly or wrongly. This redirected generosity anticipates the cheerful giving Paul commends among believers building up the true temple of God's people.
basic.christ: Paul writes that God loves a cheerful giver, 2 Corinthians 9:7, echoing exactly the willing-hearted giving described throughout this chapter. The skilled craftsmen equipped to build God's earthly dwelling anticipate the gifted members of Christ's body, equipped by the same Spirit to build up the church, His true temple, Ephesians 4:11-13.
basic.god: God does not merely command Israel's generosity; He first stirs their hearts, so that giving flows from willingness rather than obligation. He also multiplies His gifting, giving not only skill to Bezalel and Oholiab but the further ability to teach, ensuring His work is carried forward by many rather than a gifted few.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Corinthians 9:7 says God loves a cheerful giver. Ephesians 4:11-13 describes gifted members equipping the whole body of Christ for the work of ministry. 1 Peter 4:10 calls every believer to use whatever gift they have received to serve others.
basic.oneStory: Israel's hearts, once misdirected toward a golden calf, are now stirred to overflowing generosity for God's true dwelling place, and the Spirit equips craftsmen not only with skill but the ability to teach others. This pattern of Spirit-stirred, willing giving and Spirit-multiplied gifting continues in the church, the true temple, built up by every believer's freely offered gifts.

context.literary: This chapter marks the turn from instruction to execution: everything described in chapters 25 through 31 as command now becomes, through the people's willing response, material actually gathered and craftsmen actually named and ready to build.
context.historical: The overwhelming generosity described here, so much that Moses later has to restrain further giving in chapter 36, stands in sharp contrast to the golden calf episode just two chapters earlier, showing genuine repentance expressed through redirected devotion.
context.canonical: This pattern of willing, Spirit-stirred giving for God's dwelling place echoes forward into the New Testament's teaching on cheerful giving and spiritual gifts used to build up the church as God's true temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Israel's genuine, willing-hearted response to God's call after the golden calf, showing true repentance expressed through redirected generosity, and to highlight the Spirit's gifting of specific craftsmen for the practical work ahead.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical fundraising and mobilization for Israel's tabernacle; it does not prescribe a fixed pattern for church giving campaigns today. Its abiding truth is that God delights in willing, Spirit-stirred generosity and equips His people's varied skills for building up His dwelling place.

debates.question: Does this chapter's emphasis on 'whoever has a willing heart' teach that giving to God's work should always be entirely voluntary, with no expectation or structure at all?

views.label: Voluntary giving as the ideal pattern
views.case: Many interpreters see this passage, alongside 2 Corinthians 9:7, as establishing that giving to God's work is meant to flow from a freely stirred heart rather than compulsion, making this freewill offering a positive model for Christian giving generally.

views.label: Voluntary alongside other required giving
views.case: Others note that Israel also had required tithes and offerings elsewhere in the law, so this passage's voluntary character was specific to this particular building project, not a claim that all giving under the law was optional; the New Testament likewise combines cheerful, voluntary giving with expectations of regular, sacrificial generosity.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that the heart behind giving matters deeply to God, whether or not every instance of giving in Scripture was strictly voluntary; willingness and cheerfulness are consistently commended as the right spirit behind any giving.

study.originalLanguages: The phrase nadiv lev, a willing or stirred heart, appears repeatedly through the chapter, marking true worship as flowing from inward motivation rather than mere external command, in sharp contrast to Israel's earlier hasty giving toward the golden calf. Bezalel and Oholiab receive not just skill but horaah, the capacity to teach, ensuring the Spirit's gifting would be passed on rather than kept to themselves.

typology.type: Israel's overflowing, willing-hearted generosity for the tabernacle
typology.fulfillment: The cheerful, Spirit-prompted giving God loves among His people, 2 Corinthians 9:7.

typology.type: Bezalel and Oholiab, given skill and the ability to teach others
typology.fulfillment: The varied gifts of the Spirit given to build up the whole body of Christ, Ephesians 4:11-13, 1 Peter 4:10.

study.christ: Paul's teaching that God loves a cheerful giver, one who gives not reluctantly or under compulsion but from a heart moved, directly echoes the spirit of this chapter's repeated nadiv lev, willing heart, 2 Corinthians 9:7. The Spirit's gifting of Bezalel and Oholiab, distributed for the building of God's dwelling and passed on through teaching, anticipates the gifts Christ gives His church so that the whole body may be built up together, Ephesians 4:11-13.
study.god: God does not merely demand generosity; He first stirs the hearts of His people so that their giving flows freely rather than under compulsion, and He multiplies His gifts by equipping some not only with skill but the further ability to teach others, ensuring His work is carried forward broadly rather than narrowly.

crossReferences.note: God loves a cheerful giver, echoing this chapter's repeated language of a willing heart.
Christ gives gifted members to equip the saints and build up His body.
Each believer is to use whatever gift received to serve others as good stewards of God's grace.

study.oneStory: After the golden calf, Israel's hearts are stirred to overflowing, willing generosity for God's true dwelling place, and the Spirit equips craftsmen with both skill and the ability to teach others, multiplying the work. That same pattern, Spirit-stirred giving and Spirit-distributed gifts, continues in the church today, as believers freely offer their gifts to build up the true temple of God's people.

quiz.q: What did Moses restate to the whole congregation at the beginning of this chapter?

options.text: The command to keep the Sabbath, including not lighting a fire on that day.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 record this restated command.

options.text: The full list of the Ten Commandments.
options.note: Only the Sabbath command is restated here specifically, not the full Decalogue.

options.text: The instructions for the priestly garments only.
options.note: The garments are addressed later in the chapter's offering list; the opening restatement concerns the Sabbath.

options.text: Nothing was restated to the congregation.
options.note: Verses 1 through 3 explicitly restate the Sabbath command.

quiz.q: Who was invited to bring an offering for the tabernacle, according to verse 5?

options.text: Everyone whose heart was willing.
options.note: Verse 5 specifies this exact, voluntary condition for giving.

options.text: Only the tribe of Levi.
options.note: The offering was requested from the whole congregation of Israel, not the Levites alone.

options.text: Only the wealthiest families.
options.note: The invitation was based on willingness of heart, not wealth or social status.

options.text: No one specific group is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly specifies everyone whose heart was willing.

quiz.q: What specific skilled work did the women of Israel contribute, according to verses 25 to 26?

options.text: They spun blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, fine linen, and goat hair with their own hands.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 describe this exact skilled contribution from the women.

options.text: They engraved the onyx stones for the ephod.
options.note: The leaders brought the onyx stones, verse 27; the women's specific contribution described here is spinning yarn and goat hair.

options.text: They built the wooden frames of the tabernacle.
options.note: The frame construction fell to Bezalel and the skilled craftsmen; the women's described work here is spinning.

options.text: No specific contribution from women is mentioned.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 explicitly describe the women's skilled spinning.

quiz.q: What two abilities did God give Bezalel and Oholiab, according to verses 34 to 35?

options.text: Skill in craftsmanship and the ability to teach others.
options.note: Verse 34 specifically adds the ability to teach alongside their craftsmanship skill.

options.text: Wealth and political authority.
options.note: Their gifting concerned craftsmanship and teaching ability, not wealth or political power.

options.text: Prophetic insight and military strength.
options.note: Their described gifts are practical craftsmanship and teaching, not prophecy or military skill.

options.text: No specific abilities are described.
options.note: Verses 34 to 35 explicitly describe their craftsmanship and teaching abilities.

quiz.q: What kind of offering did the people bring, according to the repeated description in verses 21 and 29?

options.text: A freewill offering, brought by those whose hearts and spirits were stirred.
options.note: Both verses use this exact language of willing, spirit-prompted giving.

options.text: A mandatory tax assessed by tribal leaders.
options.note: The text explicitly emphasizes voluntary, heart-stirred giving, not a mandatory tax.

options.text: A single, one-time gift from Moses alone.
options.note: The offering came from the whole community, men and women together, not from Moses alone.

options.text: No offering was actually brought.
options.note: Verses 21 through 29 describe extensive, generous giving from the whole congregation.

questions: The people's hearts were stirred to give generously for the tabernacle, in sharp contrast to their earlier eager giving toward the golden calf. Where might your own generosity be currently directed toward the wrong things, and what would it look like to redirect it toward God?
Both men and women brought their skills and possessions freely for this work. What skills or resources has God given you that you have not yet offered willingly for His purposes?

Bezalel was given not only skill but the ability to teach others, multiplying his gift rather than keeping it to himself. Where do you have knowledge or ability you could be passing on to others rather than keeping for yourself alone?
So much was given that the offering eventually had to be restrained. What would need to change in your own giving for it to become this kind of overflowing, willing generosity?

God loves a cheerful giver, one who gives from a stirred heart rather than compulsion. Honestly examine your own giving, whether of money, time, or skill: does it flow more from willingness or from obligation?

translationNotes.note: Do not light a fire in any of your dwellings on the Sabbath day is a specific application of the general Sabbath rest command, likely addressing the labor involved in gathering fuel and tending a fire, later debated among Jewish interpreters regarding its exact scope.

Both men and women brought offerings, and the text specifically notes women's skilled spinning in verses 25 to 26, showing that the whole community, not merely male heads of households, participated directly in this work.

The ability to teach others, using a Hebrew root related to instruction, torah, shows that Bezalel and Oholiab's gifting was intended to be multiplied through others they trained, not confined to their own individual craftsmanship.
